title: Arkhaven Medical Platforms
preview:
Background: Arkhaven runs a clinical triage + patient Q&A layer across several hospital networks. Currently on Redwood Hosted API with steady growth (1.2B tokens/mo). Primary drivers for expansion: predictable latency for emergent triage workflows, data residency for EMR-integrated sites, and unit-cost reduction for large-batch summarization pipelines.

Commercial ask: 1) Pilot Dedicated (3x A100-equivalent pool) for latency-critical triage and trial matching; 2) Adopt Redwood Optimize for batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization recommendations to reduce token spend and GPU-hours; 3) Add optional Private (on-prem package) for two hospital partners in Midwest with air-gapped requirements.

Security: Requested SSO/SAML, KMS integration (customer-managed key), SOC2 + ISO27001 evidence. Legal requested a KMS amendment and data residency clause for on-prem sites. Security already consumed Redwood security FAQ and ran a questionnaire (see drive doc).

Quote from CTO (Dr. Leena Rao): "We need predictable tail latency under 120ms for triage — variability today is the killer. If Dedicated + Optimize hits the numbers, we can consolidate three internal inference stacks into Redwood."

Operational notes: Current usage pattern: mix of chat-like triage requests (low token per request, high qps) and large batch summarization for daily records (high token volumes). Proposed routing: route triage to Dedicated pool, fallback to hosted variants during burst. Require regional presence in EU for an upcoming M&A subsidiary.

POC success metrics: latency p95 < 150ms (triage), cost per 1M tokens reduced by >=30% after Optimize pilot, integration with customer KMS for both Hosted/Dedicated.

Sales/ops notes: AE Maya + SE Ethan ran sizing exercise; initial Dedicated quote shared 2026-02-22. CSM Nina leading adoption plan. Legal flagged KMS wording 2026-03-10 (blocking signature).

title: Deepwell KnowledgeGrid
preview:
Summary: Large financial analytics provider building RAG over internal research + regulatory filings. Target index 200M–800M vectors initially; plan growth to 1B+ within 12–18 months. Primary workload: embeddings + two-stage rerank (ANN -> cross-attention reranker).

Key constraints: cannot send PII or regulated doc content to third-party public cloud. Customer wants Private deployment or on-prem with customer-managed KMS and full audit logging. Enterprise SLAs required.

Performance & cost targets: p95 rerank latency <=150ms, p99 <=300ms. Throughput target 1,000 qps peak (avg 250 qps). Cost sensitivity: unit cost target $0.0005–$0.002 per query depending on rerank depth; looking to minimize GPU token cost via batching, prefix/KV caching and 8-bit quantization.

POC scope: ingest 50M vectors subset -> validate ANN sharding, hybrid routing, and rerank latency at target QPS; run A/B against in-house baseline for quality vs cost. Deliverables asked: prescriptive batching/caching configs, quantization guidance, cost model for 200M/500M/1B vectors, and a network peering + encryption plan.

Quotes: "Need unit cost under 0.001/token equivalent for reranking at 500M index scale" — Head of Data.

SE notes: Recommend continuous batching with dynamic batch sizing, prefix cache for repeated queries during market opens, and autoscale rules for burst windows. Expect ~40% cache hit on common prefixes. Prefer GPU-backed Dedicated in-region, fallback to Private across other regions for compliance.

Risks: procurement calendar (decision Q2), long security review (legal wants HSM/KMS details), and on-prem infra readiness for 1B vector sharding.

Next steps: send prescriptive config + cost sheet by 2026-03-15; schedule technical deep-dive for peering + KMS on 2026-03-18.

title: Embercoda Labs
preview:
Lead: Nina Park (CTO). Small devtools startup building an IDE-integrated code assistant + repo search. Wants Hosted API (self-serve) — fast onboarding is key. Primary asks: 1) long context support (claims they need ~64k token windows for whole-repo snippets), 2) clear rate limit tiers and burst behavior (expected low QPS but spiky during CI/IDE events), 3) example SDK snippets for Node + Python showing streaming + function calling for code completions, 4) deterministic cost estimates for batching files and embeddings.

Recent call notes:
- "We need to surface whole-file context and preserve cross-file references when generating completions." — Nina.
- They ran a quick PoC against competitor X (small window) and want to compare latency and cost on longer contexts.
- Latency SLAs: interactive in-editor <300ms for short prompts; acceptable up to ~1s for heavy context completion. Throughput: baseline 2-4 req/s per seat, could spike to ~20 req/s during internal test runs.
- Rate-limit concern: want explicit per-token and per-minute limits + guidance on exponential backoff and retry semantics.
- SDK asks: Node + Python examples, code to stream tokens into Monaco editor, examples for chunked embeddings and prefix caching.
- Security: standard SaaS — SSO (SAML), audit logs, retention controls. No air-gapped/on-prem requirement at this stage.
- Budget: early-stage, price sensitive — expecting a starter committed tier or strong free credit runway. Estimated initial ARR $5k–$20k if economics look good.

Stubs/refs:
- Fireflies call id: ff_20260301_98234
- Drive: rw-pricing-proposal-v1.pdf (internal draft shared)
- Jira: RW-3812 opened to capture rate-limit doc ask.

Sales posture: PLG → AE-assisted (self-serve first, SE call to unblock technical reqs). Good fit for Hosted API; Dedicated/Private not needed now. Next: provide 10k/64k token example snippets + a concise doc that maps batching/cache knobs to cost delta. Follow up w/ SE tech call week of Mar 22.

title: Inkwell Codeworks
preview:
Top-line: Inkwell builds an AI-augmented IDE and CI pipeline assistant for mid-market SaaS teams. Interested in Redwood to power low-latency streaming completions in the IDE and a function-calling workflow to run tests/lints/builds inside isolated sandboxes. Key asks: model/version pinning, predictable latency for editor streaming, and auditable execution logs for security/compliance.

Discovery notes:
- 2025-11-12: Intro call (Ethan P. + Priya) — product fit looks strong, they want hosted for eval then private for production.
- 2026-01-18: Technical deep-dive (SE Priya) — talked about function/tool calling: agent sends payload -> sandbox container executes tests/lint -> returns structured test results and artifacts (logs, coverage). They want the "tool call" to include provenance metadata.
- 2026-02-21: Demo (ff_2026-02-21_inkwell_meeting) — showed streaming latencies goal 50-120ms per token in editor; demoed pinning to a stable v1.4 model for code completion. Positive on automatic fallback to smaller model when CPU constrained.
- 2026-03-02: Security walkthrough (ff_2026-03-02_security_walkthrough) — discussed KMS integration, audit logging retention (90 days min), SAML SSO, and air-gapped option for enterprise customers.

POC scope proposed (2 weeks):
- Objective: Validate function-calling for test-runner + lint integration with safe sandboxing and streamed completion in the IDE.
- Deliverables: 1) Docker-based sandbox template + resource limits, 2) end-to-end flow: editor->agent->tool-call->sandbox->structured result, 3) latency and cost report, 4) harness to replay failing tests for regression checks.
- Success criteria: passing test-run POC pipeline without manual intervention, median token streaming latency < 120ms in NA region, reproducible artifact collection + audit logs.

Concerns voiced by Inkwell (shorthand):
- "We cannot have arbitrary outbound egress from sandbox containers unless approved."
- "Need cryptographic proof of model version pinned for audits."
- "Budget is constrained during POC — want hard spend cap."

Commercial / procurement notes:
- Procurement expects a small-term POC SOW then expand to a 12mo private deployment if POC meets criteria. Finance asked for indicative pricing deck (linked). Legal flagged remote code execution clause — will require indemnity language.

Next steps (short):
- Send updated POC SOW with spend cap and explicit sandbox egress policy (Ethan) — target 2026-03-12.
- Arrange SSO test window with identity team (Priya/Jasper) — target 2026-03-18.
- Provide sample audit log schema + model pinning proof format (SE deliverable).

Quick quotes paraphrased:
- "We need the agent to call a test runner without exposing infra — sandboxing is non-negotiable."
- "Pin the model for production — can't have drifting completions."

Internal notes for Redwood team:
- Consider recommending Redwood Optimize batch settings for background CI-style agents to reduce token cost while preserving interactive editor latency via hybrid routing.
- Potential upsell: Dedicated pool for heavy CI workloads + Private for tenant isolation.
- Risks: Legal + sandbox isolation architecture may push timeline to procurement if not clarified quickly.

title: BriefFoundry Legal Assist
preview:
Lead source: inbound signup (self-serve). Trial account created 2026-02-18 using free credits. Early discovery with CEO (Oliver Grant) + Head of Engineering (Priya Rao). Key context: small legaltech startup building contract summarization and internal advisor chat for SMB law firms. They ingest client contracts (confidential), sometimes PII. Prefer US-only data residency and want explicit audit logging for every request.

Highlights from call 2026-02-19 (ff_20260219_brieffoundry_intro):
- "We cannot have customer docs leave the US boundary; legal team will block otherwise." (Oliver)
- Interested in embeddings for semantic search across contracts + chat assistant for paralegals.
- Did quick smoke test of hosted API; liked generation quality but flagged two concerns: request/response logging and KV/prefix caching behavior.
- Asked if Redwood can disable request logging or provide redaction hooks and how long logs are retained by default. Wants retention policy that can be set per-account (legal wants 2-7 years for auditability but with ability to purge specific records).
- Wants confirmation that API keys and request payloads are not used for model training and options to route traffic to a US region endpoint.

Technical notes (SE follow-up items):
- Engineer asked about KV cache persistence: are prefixes stored long-term? Need doc on caching lifecycle and controls to opt-out.
- Request for example policy showing how to disable logging + enable audit logs to separate secure S3 with restricted access.
- Cost sensitivity: "Keep cost predictable — token-level billing + per-route dashboards would help us allocate spend to customer projects."

Behavioral / GTM notes:
- Self-serve first; likely to remain SMB with modest ARR but could expand if Dedicated offered later.
- Timeline: Legal review expected 2-3 weeks once we provide security FAQ + retention options. Forecasting conservative close if legal satisfied.

Stated next actions (recorded):
1) Send tailored security FAQ and short note on US-region endpoints and data residency (owner: Maya) — by 2026-03-06.
2) SE (Ethan) to demo audit-log config + show example quickstart that disables request logging and demonstrates per-route cost breakdown — schedule 60m deep-dive.
3) Provide sample DPA snippet and redaction/retention policy template.

Quote from Priya (engineer): "If we can control what gets logged and pin our data to us-east, we can move quickly — otherwise legal won't sign off."

title: AxonField Supportware
preview:
Summary: mid-market SaaS for customer support teams. Pushing knowledge-base RAG into support UI (in-app answer suggestions + agent assist). Primary asks: high QPS (sustained ~200-300 qps), predictable per-query cost, and sub-150ms 95th latency for top-k rerank flow. Currently prototyping with third-party vector store + OpenAI embeddings; want single vendor for hosted embedding + rerank to simplify SLAs and lower egress/cost.

Key customer constraints (from calls):
- Index size: ~6-12M doc chunks (moderate scale).
- Query pattern: short queries, many concurrent users, spikes during support events.
- Cost sensitivity: wants per-query model to be ~<$0.006 median to justify replacing current provider.
- Security: SSO (SAML), SOC2 required, audit logs + configurable retention. Data residency for EU customers (subset of customers).

title: Archway VPC Integrations Ltd.
preview:
Prospect: regional fintech (payments + risk). Very strict data residency requirements (primary in DE + FR). Primary ask: Redwood Private deployed into Archway-managed VPC with no public egress, direct connectivity (AWS PrivateLink or peering/TGW), and HSM-backed KMS for key material.

Network specifics captured during workshop: prefer PrivateLink for cross-account access but networking team concerned about endpoints per AZ and cost at scale. Alternative: VPC peering via Transit Gateway with strict route table controls. Must support IP allowlists, per-subnet firewall rules, and ability to lock down egress to a small set of controlled NAT/egress proxies.

Security review status: questionnaire 70% complete. Pending items: HSM/KMS key custody model confirmation (customer-managed CMKs vs Redwood-managed), audit_log retention window (they require 7 years for some flows), and proof of no model weights leaving the private environment. Archway wants explicit guarantee that model compute and KV caches remain in-customer VPC for Private deployment.

POC notes: running embeddings rerank pipeline against sanitized transaction logs; target p50 latency for reranking < 120ms, p95 < 350ms, throughput ~250 req/s for peak windows. Cost sensitivity: mid-high (willing to pay for guaranteed throughput and residency). Preferred model family: quantized open models (Llama-derivatives or similar) hosted inside VPC, with automated fallback to smaller local model variant if capacity constrained.

Quotes / paraphrases: "We cannot have any customer PII leave EU perimeter"; "Need technical doc for network firewall rules and exact egress CIDRs by 2026-03-15".

Operational asks: integration with Archway KMS/HSM (YubiHSM or cloud HSM), centralized audit logs shipped to Archway SIEM (syslog/S3), optional long-term retention (7 years) for selected audit events, and SSO via SAML with JIT provisioning.

Next practical steps: provide network runbook (PrivateLink vs peering pros/cons + cost estimate), KMS/HSM integration pattern docs, sample firewall/iptables templates, and updated SOW reflecting a Private deployment with 3 AZ redundancy. AE note: push for security review sign-off in next 2 weeks to keep April close.

title: Catalyst Pylon Search
preview:
Summary: mid-market product analytics SaaS evaluating Redwood for embeddings + semantic search. Started with Hosted API (fast test), moving to Dedicated for predictable throughput and lower unit cost. Team: CTO (Anya Venkatar), Head of Search Eng (Caleb Moore), Data Eng lead (Rina Patel).

POC scope and ask: 2-week hosted-API POC to validate embedding quality + ingestion friction. Follow-on Dedicated trial to run throughput tests and pricing bake. Key metrics: embedding ingestion throughput (batch and streaming), end-to-end query latency (target <80ms for top-k retrieval), nightly batch job ingest 1M rows within a 3-hour window, cost per 1M embeddings. Vector DB integration must support hybrid search and similarity+filtering by metadata.

Tech notes from calls:
- Currently using Postgres + pgvector for small workloads; testing Pinecone demo for latency. Want to avoid re-architecting if Redwood can support Postgres-compatible workflows or provide clear connectors.
- Preference for open models where possible (Llama/Falcon family) with recommended quantized variants. Interested in Redwood Optimize suggestions for batching and KV caching.
- Data residency: keep indexed metadata in us-east-1, must support KMS-managed keys for PII fields.
- Security questionnaire submitted 2026-02-20 (see drive). SOC2 required, plus SSO/SAML and audit logs for admin actions.

Commercial/ops:
- Price sensitivity: moderate — willing to commit to Dedicated if unit economics show 25-40% savings vs hosted at target throughput.
- Procurement window: May 2026. Forecast to close Apr 2026 if Dedicated pricing aligns.

Quotes and shorthand from meetings:
- "Need a sample night ingest run with 1M vectors — if that fails we can't commit" — Caleb M.
- "Latency for discovery queries needs to be consistently under 100ms at 99p" — Anya V.

Action items on Redwood side:
- Provide Dedicated pricing tiers with committed throughput and burst policy examples.
- Run parallel throughput test on Hosted API: 200 qps sustained for embedding calls + 1M batch ingest simulation (link test harness).
- Confirm connectors for pgvector export/import and recommended vector DB partners for long-term ops.

title: Boltweave Real-Time Systems
preview:
- Company: fleet-scale mobility platform (ride-hail + logistics). Very cost sensitive at scale; forecasting heavy peak windows (08:00–10:00, 17:00–19:00 local).
- Primary workloads: conversational in-car assistant (short multi-turn chat), route re-ranking for ETA/traffic, and rapid summarization of driver reports.
- Latency targets: p50 < 30ms, p95 <= 120ms end-to-end for chat assistant (edge device + service). Throughput target: sustained 4k–6k req/s during peak, spiky bursts up to 12k req/s.
- Current stack: self-hosted on mixed GPU fleet (A100 and older T4s); using naive batching. Unit cost is high, and cold-starts hurt SLAs.
- Requirements called out repeatedly on calls: VPC/private control plane, KMS integration, audit logs with 1y retention, and EU data residency for certain accounts.
- Interest areas with Redwood: intelligent dynamic batching with latency SLOs, prefix/KV caching for repeated route prompts, per-model quantization profiles (4-bit/8-bit) to reduce cost on Dedicated pools, and Redwood Optimize recommendations for batching/caching/quantization knobs.
- Quote from CTO (02/26 call): "If Redwood can shave 20–30% off per-token cost while keeping p95 under 120ms we can move committed capacity."
- POC plan discussed: 2-week controlled benchmark: baseline (current infra) vs Redwood Dedicated (pinned model + quantized variant) with synthetic and sampled production traces. Include cold/warm cache scenarios.
- Security: completed initial questionnaire; next is architecture review with security team (drive: security_faq + encryption_design).
- Logistics: AE/Maya to introduce procurement team in next meeting; SE will scope benchmarking test cases.
- Helpful artifacts: pricing_deck_v2 (drive), security_faq_0301.pdf (drive), transcript Fireflies ff_20260215_9b3f2c.
- Short-hand CRM fragments: POC start target: 2026-03-10; "must hit p95 <= 120ms" reiterated; budget guardrails: prefer monthly committed pricing or reserved Dedicated for peak blocks.

title: VerityLoop Analytics
preview:
Summary: Mid-market SaaS (B2B support automation) evaluating Hosted -> Dedicated. Trial showed product-fit for RAG-enabled support agent + doc search. Primary concern: predictable latency at peak and EU residency.

Trial observations (hosted):
- Duration: 30 days. Tokens used: ~310k.
- Avg requests per minute (trial avg): 420 rpm (~7 QPS).
- Peak observed: 4,800 rpm (~80 QPS) during support surge window (Tue 14:00-15:00 ET).
- Typical concurrency: 60 concurrent sessions baseline, spikes to ~180 during incidents.
- Token mix observed: ~30% short (<64 tokens), ~50% medium (64-512), ~20% long (512-1500). Prompts avg ~140 tokens, responses avg ~360 tokens => ~500 tokens/request.

Requirements stated by customer:
- P99 latency target: <350ms for responses up to 512 tokens; degrade gracefully for long generations.
- Throughput: support 80 QPS sustained for 10-minute windows; handle 180 concurrent streams with streaming enabled.
- Cost sensitivity: mid-market budget; open to quantization/8-bit variants if quality holds.
- Routing: prefer EU region for EU customers, fallback to NA if capacity constrained.
- Security: SSO (SAML), SOC2 compliance evidence, customer-managed KMS for keys, retention controls, audit logs.

POC asks for Dedicated sizing:
- Baseline forecast: 15 QPS sustained, 80 QPS peak (10-min), concurrency 60 baseline / 180 peak.
- Token forecast (peak): 80 QPS * 60 sec * 500 tokens = 2.4M tokens/min at peak -> ~144M tokens/hour (10-min windows considered).
- Suggested Dedicated starter: 4 x A100-class GPUs with autoscale headroom for 2x bursts OR 8 x T4 equivalent for cost-optimized workloads with slower p99.
- Optimizations requested: batching policy, prefix/KV caching for active sessions, model quantization profile for cheaper variants, and per-route fallbacks to a cheaper 8-bit model for non-critical paths.

Quotes/feedback: "We liked the observability — token-level cost view was eye-opening." AE notes: procurement timeline tied to June Q2 budget cycle.

Open items: detailed GPU-hours estimate, SLO language for latency in contract, EU residency assurance, and final pricing sheet.

title: Copperstar DataWorks
preview:
Summary:
- Mid-market product analytics SaaS. B2B product with heavy in-app chat + semantic search + personalization reranker.
- Interested in hosted API for getting live quickly, then Dedicated reserved GPU once traffic is steady.

Recent timeline (bulleted):
- 2026-01-28: Intro call (AE Maya Chen) — product goals + metrics. "We need predictable latency for live support and cheaper embeddings for index ops."
- 2026-02-10: Technical deep-dive w/ SE (Ethan) — shared sample traffic profile, token distribution, avg seq len ~120 tokens.
- 2026-02-25: Pricing ask — engineering wants cost per 1M tokens and Dedicated reserved options.
- 2026-03-01: Cost optimization workshop (1.5h) run by Redwood Optimize team. Covered batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization tradeoffs. Notes captured in Drive doc.
- 2026-03-03: Internal Copperstar infra assessment — preference for VPC + KMS; EU residency for 10% of customers required.

POC / Evaluation scope:
- 2-week POC on Hosted API followed by short Dedicated pilot.
- Goals: achieve avg chat latency <200ms p95, embedding index cost reduction >=35% vs baseline, and reranking throughput 3x baseline.
- POC plan: enable prefix caching for common onboarding flows, tune batching window for 50-150ms, test int8 quantized model variant for cost/latency tradeoff.

Cost workshop highlights (actionable):
- Batching: recommended dynamic batching with 80ms window for chat peak hours — modeling shows ~22% token cost reduction while keeping p95 ~210ms (will push for further kernel optimizations).
- Caching: prefix caching on onboarding flows could remove 12-18% of token usage; need to define cache invalidation policies for privacy-sensitive fields.
- Quantization profiles: try 4-bit quantization on reranker model to cut inference GPU memory by ~40% and reduce per-token cost; signal-check to ensure negligible quality drift on top-10 rerank.
- Unit economics: modeled with Copperstar's traffic (350k monthly active users): combined optimizations project 30-45% lower monthly spend; break-even on Dedicated reserved capacity expected at ~$12k/month committed.

Security / compliance notes:
- Copperstar requires SSO (SAML) + SOC2 and audit logging for initial contract. KMS integration for key management mandatory for Dedicated.
- EU data residency: need private routing for EU subset or Redwood hosted region guarantee.

Quotes / call snippets:
- "If we can get predictable p95 and lower embedding cost, we can justify moving to Dedicated." — Head of Product
- "Show me actual unit economics with our traffic, not generic numbers." — CFO (finance wants POC cost model).

Open items / blockers (short):
- Security team needs architecture diagram + pen-test evidence (SEC-2098).
- Legal needs standard DPA + data residency annex.
- Finance to confirm commit level for Dedicated pilot.

Next steps (detailed):
- 2026-03-05: Deliver POC runbook + cost model (AE/SE).
- 2026-03-10: Workshop w/ infra + product to run optimization tuning; include Redwood Optimize engineer.
- 2026-03-17: Start Hosted API POC (2 weeks).
- After POC: analyze telemetry (tokens broken down by route) and propose Dedicated commit sizing + pricing tier.

Notes style: CRM shorthand, action-items, links to artifacts above.

title: Emberwood Capacity Analytics
preview:
Summary:
- Evaluation focused on Dedicated capacity economics + latency SLOs for multi-tenant adserving pipelines. Primary ask: predictable throughput for 1st-party signal enrichment and real-time reranking in auctions.

Timeline / recent activity:
- 2026-01-28: Intro call (Ethan / Lina / infra) — high level requirements, agreed to cost modeling exercise.
- 2026-02-18: Deep dive (Fireflies ff_20260218_emberwood_call_7a9d) — provided baseline traffic profiles and peak QPS windows.
- 2026-02-25: Security intake submitted (SEC-3098).
- 2026-03-01: Sizing workshop with SE (Lina) — settled on 3 commitment scenarios for POC: small/med/large.

Workload and SLOs (customer-provided):
- Workloads: chat assist for internal growth ops (20% traffic), synchronous reranking (40%), embeddings for search and feature store (40%).
- Latency targets: reranking p95 <= 120ms (2k token context), chat p95 <= 200ms (avg 1k token).
- Throughput target: steady-state 180 QPS for reranking during peak; burst capacity to 350 QPS for 5-10 mins.
- Reliability: SLA expectation 99.9% uptime, region failover within 60s for active routes.

Reserved capacity cost model we presented (high-level):
- Assumptions: using H100-equivalent GPUs; on-demand unit cost baseline = $X/hr (redacted in deck); batching + KV cache expected to raise effective throughput by ~2.2x.
- Commitment scenarios modeled (monthly committed GPUs):
  1) Small: 6 GPUs (developer -> early production). Assumed avg util 60%, peak 85%. Estimated blended token cost ≈ $0.00055 per generated token (incl infra & SW ops). Monthly committed discount vs on-demand ~35%.
  2) Medium: 12 GPUs (production baseline). Assumed avg util 70%, peak 95% (with burst pool). Estimated blended token cost ≈ $0.00037/token. Monthly discount ~55% vs on-demand.
  3) Large: 24 GPUs (high-throughput / reserve). Assumed avg util 75% with aggressive caching. Estimated blended token cost ≈ $0.00026/token. Monthly discount ~65% vs on-demand.
- Example math excerpt (included in deck):
  - Baseline token throughput per GPU (with quantized model + batching) = T tokens/sec/GPU (customer-specific); monthly effective token capacity = T * 3600 * 24 * 30 * util. Divide committed monthly cost by effective tokens to get $/token.
- Sensitivity: unit economics heavily sensitive to util ( +/-10% util -> +/-20-30% per-token cost).

POC plan / technical milestones:
- POC scope: 72-hour sustained load on dedicated 12-GPU cluster (H100-equivalent) + synthetic traffic matching peak mix (60% reranking, 30% chat, 10% embeddings).
- Metrics to capture: p50/p95/p99 latency by route, tokens/sec, GPU utilization, batch sizes, cache hit rate, end-to-end tail latency under burst.
- SE tasks: pin model variants (full-fp16 and 4-bit quant), enable prefix/KV caching, configure autoscaler (burst pool), setup tracing hooks to Console.
- Timeline: kickoff 2026-03-10, run POC 2026-03-17 to 2026-03-20, internal review 2026-03-23.

SLA & negotiation notes:
- Customer requests: 99.95% uptime for Dedicated, p95 latency crediting banded (if p95 > target for >15 min window -> service credit). Wants explicit fallback model family & region routing detailed in contract.
- Redwood constraints: standard Dedicated SLA is 99.9% w/ 15% service credit tiers; legal to review 99.95% ask with additional commercial commitment.
- Escalation: SE + CSM to own operational playbook; require runbook & joint run-calls during POC.

Open questions / blockers:
- Exact token accounting: do we bill input+output tokens or output-only for reranking workflows? (affects ARR materially).
- Confirmation of acceptable model quantization levels (customer wants fidelity check vs QA).
- Budget: procurement needs seat in Q2 budget approval; finance wants three-year TCO scenarios.

Next steps (short):
- Share detailed 3-scenario cost workbook (drive link) and POC runbook (Lina).
- Schedule kickoff for 2026-03-10 pending security gating.
- Prepare contract straw (include proposed 99.95% SLA tier + fallback commitments) for legal review.

Stubs / refs:
- Pricing deck: /drive/emberwood/EMB-Prod-Dedicated-Proposal-v2.pdf
- SLA Qs: /drive/emberwood/EMB-SLA-questions.xlsx
- Call transcript: ff_20260218_emberwood_call_7a9d

AE notes / tone: Emberwood is cost-sensitive but expects enterprise reliability. They have internal SREs for on-call integration; likely to push for 12-24 GPU commit if POC validates p95 & unit economics. Keep focus on util improvements (batching, cache) — biggest lever to hit their ARR targets.

title: Foxglove Throughput Partners
preview:
POC summary: Foxglove is evaluating Redwood Dedicated for low-latency chat + reranking across high-volume user traffic. Primary goal is a predictable Dedicated cluster with throughput guarantees and explicit latency SLOs per route. CTO quote on 2026-02-14 call: "we need p99 under 500ms for chat routes or we can't go live".

POC scope and acceptance criteria (short):
- Sustained throughput: 500 qps of short-chat requests (avg 40 tokens) with spikes to 1k qps for 2-minute bursts.
- Latency SLOs: p95 < 200ms, p99 < 500ms for chat API; reranking p95 < 120ms.
- Soak acceptance: 48-hour steady-state run at 350 qps with no SLO violations > 1% and no memory leaks.
- Throughput guarantee: reserve GPU pool sized to meet steady-state + 2x burst headroom; explicit failover to regional backup pool.

Test plan executed so far (high level):
- 2026-02-10: kickoff; drive doc with representative prompts uploaded (see linked_drive_docs).
- 2026-02-18: baseline hosted run against model-x-small to gather latency distribution under synthetic traffic. Results: sustained 200 qps, p95 220ms, p99 420ms. Notes: KV cache cold start impacted p99.
- 2026-02-24: dedicated cluster baseline (4x A100-80GB): sustained 450 qps, p95 170ms, p99 360ms. Observed token-level tail from long prefix conditioning.
- 2026-02-28: focused reranking test with batched queries (batch size 4): throughput +30%, p95 improved by ~40ms.
- 2026-03-01: preliminary soak run (6 hours) at 300 qps. No memory growth observed; CPU on control plane spikes during autoscale events.

Representative prompts used in POC: short chat support messages ("account balance discrepancy, summarize steps to resolve"), personalization ranking queries (user context + 10 candidate descriptions), and short summarization (2-3 sentence condense). Example sample prompt included in drive: 3-context history + 1-shot instruction to respond under 80 tokens.

Technical considerations / recommended config from Redwood SE:
- Reserved pool proposal: 8 x A100-80GB (reserved) for steady-state + configured burst on 2x H100 nodes in peak windows.
- Autoscaling policy: predictive scale-up on scheduled marketing blasts; manual override for less than 10-min ramp windows.
- KV/prefix caching: enable warm cache during soak to reduce cold-start tails; use shared cache between same-customer sessions.
- Quantization plan: evaluate 4-bit quantized variant of primary model for cost/throughput tradeoff; fall back to full-fp16 variant on critical routes.

SLA negotiation points outstanding:
- Define per-route SLOs (chat vs rerank) and penalty credit model for p99 violations.
- Explicit throughput guarantee in tokens/sec or max concurrent requests per cluster; acceptance of degraded throughput with automatic fallback model.
- Clarify maintenance windows and guaranteed advance notice for disruptions.

Cost and commercial notes:
- Foxglove aiming for unit token cost competitive with their current supplier; procurement requested committed discount for 12-month reserved capacity.
- Estimated ARR range provided to Redwood: $150k–$400k if SLA terms acceptable and burst allowances defined.

Next steps (detailed):
- Redwood to deliver soak-test config YAML + traffic schedule by 2026-03-05.
- Foxglove security to sign off on KMS key policy; SE to work with security team on key rotation doc (drive link above).
- Legal to review SLA draft; target procurement approval week of 2026-05-04.
- Plan 48-hour soak window (tentative) 2026-03-15 to 2026-03-17; Redwood to provide monitoring dashboard access and token-cost breakdown during run.

Contacts and call notes:
- AE Maya Chen: primary deal owner, sends weekly summary.
- SE Ethan Park: POC lead; ran dedicated tuning on 2026-02-24.
- Foxglove CTO (Liam O'Rourke): strong latency constraints; key stakeholder for SLA.
- Meeting artifacts: ff_2026_02_14_7321 (kickoff transcript), ff_2026_02_28_8112 (soak plan review).

Short CRM-style action items:
- "SE: push tuned kernel settings for long-prefix runs";
- "Legal: align SLA credit model with procurement threshold";
- "Security: confirm KMS policy + HSM requirement by 2026-03-08".

Summary assessment: POC progress is positive—dedicated cluster meets near-term p95 targets and has room to hit p99 targets after cache warmup and kernel tuning. Main risks are procurement capacity commit timeline and formalizing SLA penalty/credit structure.

title: BeaconBridge Analytics
preview:
Summary: Mid-market analytics SaaS building an in-app conversational assistant for product support and guided analytics. Primary interest in Hosted API to accelerate time-to-value; evaluating Dedicated for predictable throughput if cost/latency tradeoffs justify it.

Recent discussions: 
- "Initial discovery 2025-11-05": product + infra overview. They emphasized low engineering headcount and desire for a managed endpoint.
- "Demo 2026-01-14": hosted API streaming demo (tool calling + structured outputs). Product team liked streaming but asked about latency p95 under load.
- "POC kickoff 2026-02-01": 2-week POC (hosted) to validate latency SLOs, tool calling flow (external ticketing + DB connector), and log redaction.
- "Security questionnaire delivered 2026-02-12": they returned with follow-ups on SSO, SOC2 evidence, retention windows, and log access.
- "POC interim review 2026-02-24": observed avg first-token latency ~120ms p50, p95 ~380ms under synthetic 40 concurrent chats — above their target p95 300ms. SE adjusted batching and routing; retesting.
- "Pricing intro 2026-03-01": early pricing options shared; customer requested Dedicated sizing estimate vs hosted unit cost at 40-80 concurrent sessions.

Requirements / priorities (customer language):
- "Latency first": aiming for p95 <= 300ms for streaming first token in core agent flow. Must preserve tool call latency (<500ms end-to-end) for ticket creation.
- "Managed first, private later": test Hosted API now, open to Dedicated in 6-12 months if cost predictable.
- "Security must-haves": SSO/SAML integration (Okta), SOC2 Type II evidence, audit logs with PII redaction, ability to set retention policy 30/90/365 days, KMS integration for secrets.
- "Model / quality": prefer open chat models (fine-tuned Llama2-chat family) with automatic fallback to quantized variant for cost/concurrency. Need deterministic tool-calling reliability.

POC success criteria (agreed):
- Achieve p95 streaming first-token <= 300ms for 40 concurrent chat sessions (synthetic load).
- Demonstrate tool-calling reliability >= 99% for ticket create workflow under POC load.
- Security signoff on hosted data flow, DPA, and proof of log PII redaction (redaction scripts or pipeline).

Quotes / paraphrases: 
- "We don't want to own model infra yet — get us running in 2 weeks." — Head of Product
- "If hosted can meet latency reliably, we'll delay Dedicated but need clear route to it." — CTO

Open items: 
- Redwood to provide detailed DPA + network diagram for hosted API (security asked for flow to third-party monitoring).
- SE to run tuned config: reduce batch windows, KV cache priming, and test smaller model variant fallback.
- Finance ask: 12-month cost comparison Hosted vs Dedicated for 40/80 concurrent sustained sessions.

Where we stand: POC mid-progress, security review active, performance tuning in next sprint. High likelihood to convert if latency SLO and redaction proof satisfied.

title: Indigo AgentFlow
preview:
Summary: Indigo AgentFlow builds a hybrid agent-assist + automation layer for mid-market retail and fintech contact centers. Primary interest is robust tool-calling — create/update Zendesk tickets, issue refunds via Stripe API, trigger PagerDuty escalations, and open engineering tickets in JIRA when automated remediation fails.

Customer quotes / voice: 
- "We need the model to make outbound API calls the same way a human agent would — but fail safely. No blind refunds." — Head of Ops
- "Top priority is predictable behavior during holiday spikes; last year our webhook throttles collapsed." — CTO

Technical requirements (callouts):
- Tool-calling surface: create_ticket, update_ticket, add_comment, issue_refund, escalate_to_pagerduty, create_jira_issue. Idempotency keys required for all mutating calls.
- Integrations: Zendesk (sandbox + prod), Stripe (restricted test keys, PCI scope reduction), PagerDuty, Atlassian JIRA.
- Latency/throughput targets: ticket-create end-to-end median < 450ms, p95 < 900ms under steady 250 rps; graceful degradation under spikes to 1500 rps with fallback to async webhook + human queue.
- Reliability: target 99.95% availability for tool-calls during business-critical hours; automatic retry/backoff and circuit-breaker patterns.
- Security: SSO (SAML), granular audit logs for each tool-call, HSM-backed KMS for signing idempotency tokens, retention controls for PII removed from ephemeral caches.
- Observability: need token-level cost breakdown, per-route latency histograms, and replayable request traces for RCA.

POC plan:
- Week 0: account setup + access to Indigo sandbox Zendesk and Stripe test tenant.
- Week 1: implement basic create_ticket flow + agent-assist summarization for agent UI.
- Week 2: wire refunds flow with manual approval gating; run 1000 synthetic transactions at varied RPS to exercise autoscaling.
- Acceptance criteria: idempotent refund call verified, audit logs show full tool-call trace, no PII persisted beyond 24h in test environment, p95 ticket-create < 900ms during 250 rps test.

Recent activity (short bullets):
- 2026-02-18: Demo run with Jordan/Samira — Fireflies link above. Customer liked fallback to async webhook.
- 2026-02-22: Sent security questionnaire and PCI scoping request (drive doc).
- 2026-02-25: Engineering follow-up call with Indigo infra team to discuss KMS/HSM placement.
- 2026-02-28: Agreed to 2-week POC; signed POC SOW draft. Gmail thread above.

Concise next steps / owner responsibilities:
- AE (Jordan): finalize SOW, collect billing contact, schedule kickoff.
- SE (Samira): provision dedicated POC pool, create Zendesk/Stripe sandbox connectors, implement idempotency middleware example.
- Security (Ethan/CSM): run PCI scope checklist and confirm KMS approach.

Risk notes / blockers:
- PCI scoping still open — If scope expands we move from hosted -> dedicated/private.
- Customer insists on retention_controls and proof that LLM prompts/kv cache do not persist PII beyond configured TTL.
- Requires explicit SLA language for tool-calls (timeouts, retries, fallback behavior).

Misc links / refs: pricing-deck-v3 (drive), security-faq pdf (drive), jira keys for integration work: SUPPORT-1123 (zendesk connector), INFRA-987 (k8s autoscaling tests).

title: Helix Insights AI
preview:
Status: closed-lost (decisioned 2026-03-02). Lost to incumbent (OpenAI) on quality for structured output and tool-calling reliability.

Summary of engagement:
- 2025-11-18: Intro call (AE: Ethan Park) — Helix interested in migrating a reranking + chat layer from in-house GPT-based setup to Redwood for cost predictability and observability. Security org insisted on VPC or on-prem.
- 2025-12-02: Demo: Redwood hosted API + Optimize suggestions. Good fit on cost controls, AE ran token-level cost scenarios. SE (Maya) demoed model-routing and fallback.
- 2026-01-10: POC kickoff (2-week). Goal: match incumbent's structured JSON outputs for compliance summaries and enable reliable function/tool calls for an external risk-scoring service. Provided sample prompt set and eval metrics.
- 2026-01-20: Security questionnaire submitted, asked for on-prem deployment checklist and KMS/HSM diagrams. Security team asked for SOC2 + ISO guidance.
- 2026-01-28: Mid-POC check-in. Early red flags: Redwood model variant produced inconsistent key ordering and occasional malformed JSON; function-calling semantics sometimes omitted required fields. Logging/tracing looked good but outputs failed JSON schema validation at 12% higher rate vs incumbent.
- 2026-02-14: Deep dive with product + SEs. We tested deterministic function-calling path and structured-output constraints. SEs implemented stricter schema enforcement in prompt + post-processing suggestion, but Helix ran blind evals and reported lower precision on entity extraction compared to incumbent.
- 2026-02-21: Helix legal and security escalate — ask for on-prem timeline and SLA for private control plane. They also asked for an explicit remediation plan for model quality regressions (metric baselines, rollback policy).
- 2026-02-25: Internal budget freeze at Helix due to re-org; champion (Director of AI Platforms) put purchase on hold, then left the company (internal move) 2026-02-28. No clear replacement.
- 2026-03-02: Final decision: choose incumbent (OpenAI) due to superior out-of-the-box structured output and more mature function-calling guarantees. Also accepted that incumbent runs in their preferred VPC configuration already.

Key technical learnings / flagged items:
- Structured-output reliability was the primary differentiator. Redwood required prompt + Optimize tuning; incumbent achieved lower schema fail rate without extra engineering.
- Function/tool calling: Helix emphasized mandatory required-field guarantees and deterministic invocation ordering. Redwood's runtime had non-deterministic edge cases in the POC prompts we gave.
- Observability: Helix liked Redwood Console token-level costs and tracing — seen as a net positive but not decisive.
- Security: Helix needed on-prem option with KMS integration and SOC2 evidence — we provided docs but timeline was too long for them.

AE notes / next actions for archive:
- Capture precise failing prompt examples in repo (done, linked).
- Feed POC eval metrics back to model onboarding team and Optimize roadmap: work item to improve structured-output guidance + more robust function-calling mode.
- Re-engagement plan: target Q4 2026 after Helix stabilizes budget and hires new AI platform lead. Offer a short follow-up audit (free) focused on structured-output parity.

Customer quote snippets captured: "Output looks close but we keep getting broken JSON for edge-case invoices", "Function calls are missing fields intermittently which breaks our scoring pipeline".

Attachments: POC results PDF, security questionnaire, sample failing prompts saved in drive.

title: Acorn Financial
preview:
Account summary:
- Large enterprise financial services org. Evaluating Redwood for a mix of internal assistant + customer support summarization + doc search. Strong preference for VPC/Private deployment (hosted acceptable for early evaluation only).

Key requirements (why this deal is in the SDK parity path):
- Streaming reliability is a must-have. They see frequent mid-stream disconnects behind corporate proxies and want predictable client behavior:
  - Clear surfaced error when the stream breaks (include request_id and partial output context).
  - Guidance on what is safe to retry vs not safe to retry ("we cannot double-send an action" / avoid duplicate side effects).
  - Prefer that SDKs do NOT automatically retry mid-stream; instead expose enough metadata for app-level retries w/ idempotency.
- Strict timeout expectations:
  - They enforce hard end-to-end request budgets at their gateway (customer cited: "we kill requests at 15s unless explicitly approved").
  - Want consistent timeout knobs across Python/TypeScript/Go, including a clear overall timeout model and sane defaults.
  - Past pain point: Python streaming appeared to ignore configured timeouts (they referenced similar behavior to SUP-29505 symptoms).
- Retry behavior expectations:
  - Must respect Retry-After on 429 and back off aggressively to avoid triggering internal incident response (they have alerting on client retry storms).
  - Retryable set must be conservative and consistent (no retries on 4xx like 400/401/403). They asked for a written list of retryable codes + network failures.
  - Request_id must be present in all errors for triage (they have a runbook requiring it).

Deployment + security notes:
- Deployment: VPC preferred; private networking and KMS integration required. Data residency likely US-only for initial workloads.
- Security checklist items repeatedly mentioned:
  - SSO/SAML for console access; audit logs; retention controls; log redaction guarantees.
  - SDK auth handling: explicit parameter > env vars; consistent env var names; do not log secrets even in debug. They want this in writing.

Artifacts shared / referenced:
- Security/auth guidance shared: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/sdk-auth-resolution-and-secret-handling.json
- SDK migration notes shared: sources/confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/sdk-migration-notes-auth-retry-timeout.json
- Customer impact brief (PM-facing but used to explain rollout): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/product/hosted-api/sdk-standardization-customer-impact-brief.json
- Fireflies references:
  - 2026-01-23-redwood-technical-deep-dive-sdk-retries-and-streaming
  - 2026-02-12-security-review-auth-handling-in-sdks

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Confirm their required timeout budget for streaming vs non-streaming calls (do they allow longer for streaming?).
- Validate behavior behind their proxy with the beta SDKs (focus: Retry-After on 429, stream disconnect error shape, request_id propagation).
- Provide a short recommended config snippet per language (Python/TS/Go) that matches the new standard and includes logging fields: request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, streaming_disconnect_reason.

Internal:
- This account is a driver for the parity initiative success criteria (support volume + confidence in reliability). Keep them on the beta list once SDK releases are ready; coordinate with Ibrahim Khan (SDK parity TL) for any last-mile issues and with Marcus Lin for security signoff language.

title: Elmwood Lynx Search
preview:
Summary: Elmwood Lynx is a mid-market SaaS analytics company building semantic search into their product. Primary workloads: embeddings for nightly batch jobs (50k+ docs/day), low-latency semantic search for product UI (target 95th pct < 200ms), and occasional reranking for recommendations. Currently using a Postgres/pgvector prototype but concerned about scale and cost. Team prefers starting on Hosted API (fast onboarding) with plan to move to Dedicated once throughput and residency controls are validated.

POC specifics: ran a hosted-based POC ingest of 48k documents via Airflow -> S3 -> Redwood batch embedding jobs. Evaluated two vector DB flows: managed Pinecone (fast index ops, easier ops) vs self-managed Milvus (cheaper at scale but ops-heavy). Redwood hosted kept KV/cache behavior good during prefix-heavy query patterns; observed 95th pct latency ~120ms on demo stress test (single region).

Security & procurement: Security lead (N. Patel) asked for explicit VPC egress rules, KMS key rotation notes, and data residency language for EU/NA. Legal flagged residency clause for customer-uploaded data. Finance wants committed pricing for Dedicated (3-node reserved) before greenlighting budget.

Quotes: Product lead: "Search must feel instant — if we lose 100ms we see click-through drop." CTO: "Prefer hosted to validate UX, but must be able to move to Dedicated w/ no rewrite."

Next steps / owner actions: finish throughput POC (Ethan) by 2026-02-25, finalize vector DB decision w/ infra (Elmwood infra team) by 2026-02-28, pricing deep-dive with finance (Maya) 2026-03-05. Blockers: internal budget cycle, final security signoff on VPC egress and residency.

title: Apexhaven Intelligence
preview:
Status: STALLED — account went quiet after discovery.

Quick recap: Apexhaven is a mid-market ecommerce platform (personalization + conversational support). Primary ask: replace current ensemble (open-source + in-house) with a predictable, lower-latency serving layer that supports reranking and chat at peak traffic (p50 target ~60ms token-level, throughput ~500 concurrent chats). They prefer on-prem/VPC deployment for PII and residency.

Security: CISO escalation — asked for SOC2 evidence, KMS architecture, audit log retention policy, and a redline-able security appendix. Engineering is friendly to Redwood runtime (likes KV cache + batching suggestions) but security team flagged "no third-party key exposure" requirement.

Commercial: expected ARR band ~50k-150k; procurement indicated Q1 budget constraints — possible freeze next quarter. AE pushed Dedicated reserved capacity model. Competitors in play: Anthropic for hosted alternative, OpenAI for negotiated enterprise bundle, AWS Bedrock pilot with partner integrator (this latter came in after pricing discussion).

Why stalled: multiple factors combined — security review not closed, procurement paused due to budget cycle, and the internal champion (product lead) moved to another project. Outreach history shows engagement from engineering but not from procurement/CISO in the last 5 weeks.

Suggested approach: 1) escalate through SE to re-open technical convo with CISO (offer a 2-page redlineable appendix + short security Q&A session), 2) propose a constrained POC on a single region on Dedicated with strict KMS controls and short retention limits, 3) offer a limited-time pricing credit to bridge budget freeze.

Last quote from contact (Product lead): "If security can sign off quickly we'll re-prioritize — otherwise timing looks uncertain."

Current outreach plan: AE to follow up 2026-03-01; SE to draft security appendix by 2026-02-22. If no response by 2026-03-08 mark as closed-lost and ask for reasons (competitor, budget, security).

title: Haven Arc Trust Systems
preview:
Summary: Large defense-adjacent contact center operator evaluating Redwood Private for agent-assist and ticket summarization in a fully air-gapped environment. Primary concern is deterministic, auditable outputs and resilience during surge events.

Key requirements: minimal dependency on outbound network, ability to run in air-gapped on-prem datacenter, FIPS/HSM KMS support, audit log continuity, SSO via enterprise SAML, retention controls per DoD guidelines. Integration must sit in front of existing Genesys/Avaya ACD and call-routing stack; need tool-calling to surface backend actions (ticket creation, CRM lookups) without exposing external endpoints.

Performance targets discussed on 2026-01-15 call: 600-900 concurrent agents at peak; target 1st-token latency <250ms for agent-assist in most flows; summary generation SLA <500ms average; deterministic reranking for KB hits with explainability traces. Cost sensitivity moderate — prefer reserved capacity for predictable ARR but need private deployment pricing.

POC scope proposed: 6-week on-prem pilot in isolated DC (air-gapped): ingest 2M recent tickets for summarization evals, deploy one model variant quantized for CPU fallback, test spike generator for 2x expected peak traffic and run chaos tests. Success metrics: 90%+ summary usefulness in blind evals, <2% critical divergence from human labelers, no external egress during run.

Stakeholder quotes/highlights: “Can’t have any egress without explicit approval — that’s non-starter.” — Head of Infrastructure. “We need an auditable chain for every suggestion the agent gets.” — Contact Center Ops Lead.

Integration notes: will require custom installer for offline RPMs/containers, support for custom CA bundles, per-tenant model pinning, and policy-driven fallback (dedicated->local CPU). Red team will run a penetration and compliance validation week.

Commercial: procurement prefers 12–24 month committed capacity; ask for SOW draft and sample security addendum. Pricing deck shared (drive link).

Open technical risks: HSM integration timeline (vendor delay), on-prem GPU resource provisioning for quantized LLMs, telephony tool-calling latency under NAT/firewall rules.

Short next steps: SOW draft (Redwood) due 2026-01-30; schedule HSM scoping with customer vendor 2026-02-05; security walkthrough with network + red team week of 2026-02-12.
